name: Applicant Status
description: The veteran has applied for VR&E benefits (via VA Form 28-1900) but has not yet attended the initial orientation or entitlement determination.
details: A veteran is placed in applicant status upon receipt of a completed application. The VA is required to schedule an initial orientation and entitlement assessment under the duty to assist.
risks: Failure to attend scheduled orientation or initial counseling sessions will result in the case being discontinued before entitlement is evaluated.
advocacy: Ensure all medical records and rating decisions are submitted. Request a character of discharge review if your discharge is other than honorable.

name: Evaluation and Planning Status
description: The veteran has been found entitled to VR&E services, and is actively working with a counselor to select a vocational goal and design the rehabilitation plan (IPE).
details: This status covers the period where vocational assessments, aptitude testing, LMI research, and suitability evaluations are conducted to select a vocational goal.
risks: Counselor delay in approving goals or pushing the veteran into entry-level goals that aggravate service-connected conditions.
advocacy: Conduct independent O*NET and labor market research. Prepare justification letters if pursuing graduate school or specialized training fields.

name: Extended Evaluation Status
description: The veteran's feasibility to achieve a vocational goal is uncertain due to severe disabilities, and a structured test period (up to 12 months) is established.
details: An Extended Evaluation plan is utilized to provide therapy, accommodations, or trial coursework to test if the veteran can feasibly enter training.
risks: Counselors failing to utilize this when feasibility is uncertain, choosing instead to issue a flat unfeasibility denial.
advocacy: Request an Extended Evaluation under 38 C.F.R. § 21.74 if the counselor claims you are not feasible to train due to severe disabilities.

name: Rehabilitation to Employability (RTE) Status
description: The veteran is actively pursuing the approved rehabilitation plan (training, university courses, or OJT) to obtain the skills needed for their goal.
details: This is the active training phase. VA pays tuition, fees, subsistence allowance, and authorized supplies.
risks: Unpaid tuition delays, denied supply requests (laptops), or counselor non-response leading to class drop deadlines.
advocacy: Verify SCO certification. Maintain contact logs, and submit all required book and supplies requests prior to term starts.

name: Independent Living Status
description: A program of services (up to 24-30 months) designed to enable severely disabled veterans to function independently in their home or community.
details: For veterans where a vocational goal is currently unfeasible. Services include assistive technology, home modifications, and mobility aids.
risks: VRCs asserting that any minor work potential bars IL, or claiming local office budget caps on home adaptations.
advocacy: Complete detailed Activities of Daily Living (ADL) checklists. Rebut denials by asserting safety and community integration needs.

name: Employment Services Status
description: The veteran has completed their training and is actively searching for suitable employment, receiving job search assistance, resume prep, and interview coaching.
details: Covers active job search assistance. Veterans are eligible for up to two months of Employment Adjustment Allowance (EAA) payments.
risks: VRC closing the case into a job that is not suitable or aggravates disabilities.
advocacy: Do not accept placement in roles that violate physical or mental restrictions. Ensure any job offer satisfies suitability guidelines.

name: Interrupted Status
description: Rehabilitation services are temporarily suspended due to medical issues, personal emergencies, or communication gaps.
details: Used to pause a case before making a final decision on closure. VRC must attempt to resolve the issue before discontinuing the program.
risks: Case managers immediately placing active cases in interrupted status without warning, stopping subsistence pay.
advocacy: Provide medical documentation for any class withdrawals. Respond to interruption notices within 30 days to protect your case.

name: Discontinued Status
description: The veteran's case is closed and services are terminated prior to completing the rehabilitation goal.
details: Termination of benefits. Prior written warning notice (intent to discontinue) and formal statement of appeal rights are required.
risks: Closing cases without warning letters or omitting the mandatory VREO review for veterans rated 50% or more disabling.
advocacy: Verify if a 30-day warning was sent. Request a supervisor conference and demand a formal written Decision Notice (Form 20-0998).

name: Rehabilitated Status
description: The veteran has successfully achieved their vocational goal and maintained suitable employment for at least 60 days, or achieved independence in daily living.
details: The successful closure of a VR&E program. Indicates the veteran has overcome their employment barrier.
risks: Closing cases as rehabilitated into temporary or unsuitable jobs.
advocacy: Confirm that you have maintained the job for 60+ days and that it is fully suitable under 38 C.F.R. § 21.283 guidelines.

name: Reentrance After Rehabilitation
description: The veteran seeks to re-enter a VR&E program after previously being declared rehabilitated.
details: Re-entry is permitted if the veteran's service-connected conditions have worsened, creating a new employment barrier, or if the original job is no longer viable.
risks: VRC denying intake claiming that since you were once rehabilitated, you can never get services again.
advocacy: Obtain medical records showing worsening conditions. Prove that the previous job has become medically unsuitable.
